title: The Code of Hammurabi is inscribed
summary: Late in the reign of the Babylonian king Hammurabi, around 1754 BCE, a stele of polished black diorite more than two metres tall was erected in a temple courtyard, most probably in the sun-god's shrine at Sippar. Its curved upper face shows the enthroned Shamash handing the symbols of authority to a standing king; below runs a prologue, some 282 casuistic laws in Old Babylonian Akkadian, and an epilogue of blessings and curses. Carried off as loot to Susa in the twelfth century BCE and rediscovered by French archaeologists in 1901, it is the longest, most complete, and most influential legal text to survive from the ancient Near East.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: Map of Hammurabi's Babylonian empire at its greatest extent, c. 1750 BCE.

sections.body:
Hammurabi was the sixth king of the First Dynasty of Babylon, an Amorite line that had governed the small city on the Euphrates since around 1894 BCE. When he came to the throne in 1792 BCE (on the Middle Chronology) at the age of perhaps twenty, Babylon was one of several roughly equal states in Mesopotamia; the others included the older kingdom of Larsa in the south under Rim-Sin, Eshnunna in the Diyala valley, Mari on the middle Euphrates under Zimri-Lim, and the powerful upstart Shamshi-Adad I of Upper Mesopotamia. Hammurabi's own inscriptions and, above all, the great archive of some 25,000 clay tablets excavated by André Parrot at Mari in the 1930s allow us to watch the geopolitics of the age in unusual detail.

For his first thirty years Hammurabi was a modest player, content to build canals, restore temples, and honour his obligations to more powerful neighbours. Then, starting in his twenty-ninth regnal year (1763 BCE), he moved with extraordinary speed. He defeated Rim-Sin of Larsa and annexed the whole of southern Babylonia. Two years later he crushed a coalition of Elam, Eshnunna, and Assyria; two years after that he sacked Mari, whose great palace archives owe their preservation to the fire he set. By the end of his forty-two-year reign he ruled from the Persian Gulf to Nineveh and had reconstituted a Mesopotamian empire on a scale not seen since the Third Dynasty of Ur three centuries before.

Against this military and administrative background, in the last decade of his reign, Hammurabi commissioned the great law-stele. He was not the first Mesopotamian king to publish a legal code. Ur-Nammu of Ur around 2100 BCE, Lipit-Ishtar of Isin around 1930 BCE, and the anonymous compilers of the Laws of Eshnunna about 1770 BCE had all produced comparable, though shorter, collections. What made Hammurabi's stele unique in its own time was its length, its physical grandeur, and the vast reach of the polity it claimed to govern.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: Close-up of the relief atop the stele: Hammurabi stands before the enthroned sun-god Shamash.

sections.body:
The stele itself is a tapered pillar of black diorite, 2.25 metres tall, 79 centimetres wide at the base, and about 47 centimetres thick. On the upper face, in low relief, the king stands with his right hand raised in a gesture of prayer before the enthroned sun-god Shamash, patron of justice, who holds out the rod and ring that were the standard Mesopotamian symbols of legitimate rule. Below the relief the flat surfaces of the stele are covered on all four sides with about 4,130 lines of finely incised cuneiform, in vertical columns to be read from top to bottom, right to left. The language is Old Babylonian, a Semitic tongue closely related to Sargon's Akkadian but with the classic literary conventions of Hammurabi's age. Roughly a fifth of the text is prologue and epilogue; the remainder consists of approximately 282 legal provisions, though the numbering is modern and about 35 sections were later erased in antiquity where the Elamite conqueror planned, but never carried out, a fresh inscription.

The prologue traces Hammurabi's authority to the gods Anu, Enlil, and Marduk, and lists the shrines he has restored across his empire, from Nippur to Sippar to Nineveh. The laws themselves are cast in a distinctive if-then form (Akkadian summa awilum, 'if a man...'). They cover an extraordinary range: false witness, theft, the sale and rental of oxen, the rights of wives and concubines, adoption, medical malpractice, the wages of shipwrights and doctors, the responsibilities of tavern-keepers, the liabilities of builders whose houses collapse. Punishments vary sharply by social class. The stele recognises three main statuses: the awilum, a free citizen of some standing; the mushkenum, a person of intermediate rank, perhaps a dependent of the palace; and the wardum, a slave. The famous principle of lex talionis, 'an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth', applies among the awilum. When an awilum injures a mushkenum or a slave, the penalty is a fine paid in silver; when a slave strikes a free man, his ear is cut off.

The epilogue is a long and elaborate curse-formula against any future king who would efface Hammurabi's name from the stele or alter his laws: may Shamash break his sceptre; may Ishtar pour out his blood like water; may Nergal set the fire of pestilence in his land. Copies of the code, some of them clay-tablet extracts probably used in the training of scribes, have been recovered from a dozen sites across Mesopotamia, and it is likely that the original stele stood in the courtyard of the Ebabbar, the temple of Shamash at Sippar, where the king wished the god's justice to be seen to be done.

Around 1155 BCE, more than five centuries after Hammurabi's death, the Elamite king Shutruk-Nahhunte sacked Sippar and dragged the stele back to his capital at Susa, where it stood as a war trophy in an Elamite temple until the whole city was buried under the accumulated debris of centuries. In December 1901 and January 1902 the French archaeological mission under Jacques de Morgan, digging on the acropolis at Susa, uncovered the stele in three pieces. Reassembled, it was shipped to Paris and put on public display in the Louvre, where it remains, in the Department of Near Eastern Antiquities, catalogue number Sb 8.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
In his own kingdom Hammurabi's code seems to have functioned less as a statute book in the modern sense than as a monument of royal justice: a public advertisement, in the language of the gods, that the king was doing what a king was supposed to do. Actual court records from the Old Babylonian period, thousands of which survive, only rarely cite specific provisions of the code, and their decisions often diverge from what the stele lays down. What the stele guaranteed was not so much a specific ruling as the visible existence of a rule.

The empire Hammurabi built did not long outlast him. His son Samsu-iluna faced revolts across the south almost from his accession in 1750 BCE; by the end of his reign the ancient cities of Ur, Uruk, and Nippur had been effectively abandoned. In 1595 BCE the Hittite king Mursili I sacked Babylon itself, and the First Dynasty came to an end. The Kassite dynasty that followed ruled a smaller, less ambitious kingdom, but they preserved and copied Hammurabi's texts. Old Babylonian scribes turned the code into a schoolroom classic, and pupils in Nippur and Sippar copied out passages as writing exercises for another thousand years. In this way the code became one of the most persistently transmitted legal texts of the ancient world.

Its influence radiated far beyond Mesopotamia. The Middle Assyrian Laws of the fourteenth century BCE and the Neo-Babylonian legal compilations of the sixth century BCE clearly stand in the same tradition. Modern biblical scholars have long noted the striking parallels between the Covenant Code of Exodus 21 to 23 (a section of the Torah widely dated by scholars to the ninth or eighth century BCE) and Hammurabi's provisions, particularly the shared 'eye for an eye' formulation and the goring-ox laws. The direction of influence is debated, but a shared Mesopotamian legal culture is not. Through the Hebrew Bible, and then through Roman, canon, and common law, the categories Hammurabi's scribes cut into black stone have shaped legal thinking for more than three and a half thousand years.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The rediscovery of the stele in 1901 was one of the sensations of the young twentieth century. Vincent Scheil, the Dominican Assyriologist attached to the French mission, published the editio princeps with a French translation in 1902, only a year after the stele reached Paris. Almost overnight, the code became a fixture of ancient-history courses in Europe and North America. The American historian Robert Francis Harper's 1904 English edition was still being reprinted in the 1970s.

Hammurabi's own portrait, on the top of the stele, is one of the ways the modern world imagines a Mesopotamian king. In 1936 the United States architect Adolph Weinman placed a relief of Hammurabi among the twenty-three great lawgivers of history in the frieze of the chamber of the U.S. House of Representatives; the same figure appears in the sculpture gallery of the Supreme Court. His silhouette, hand raised before Shamash, has become an iconographic shorthand for the very idea of law.

The stele has never left Paris since 1902. A cast is displayed at the Pergamon Museum in Berlin, and other high-quality replicas circulate through the museum world. Its physical dimensions and the fine cuneiform of its columns are known to millions of visitors to the Louvre, where it stands, unrailed and floodlit, at the end of the great gallery of Near Eastern art. Above all, the code endures as a reminder of the surprising modernity of ancient thought: a written law, publicly displayed, applying differently to different classes, but applying, in principle, to everyone.

worldContext.text: In Egypt the Thirteenth Dynasty was in charge of a fragmenting Middle Kingdom, ruling from Itjtawy near modern Lisht. Royal succession had become chaotic, with dozens of short-reigned kings in less than a century, and the eastern Delta was passing into the hands of Semitic-speaking Levantine settlers whose rulers would soon proclaim themselves the Hyksos kings of the Fifteenth Dynasty. Farther south in Nubia the Kingdom of Kerma was at its height, trading gold, ebony, and ivory downriver in exchange for Egyptian manufactured goods.

In Bronze Age Europe the Minoan civilisation of Crete was in the middle of its palatial period. The great palaces at Knossos, Phaistos, and Malia, first built around 1900 BCE and destroyed in an earthquake around 1700 BCE, were being rebuilt on a larger scale. Linear A script, still undeciphered, recorded the Minoan language on clay tablets. On the mainland the shaft graves of Mycenae, with their spectacular gold masks and inlaid weapons, would begin around 1650 BCE.

In the Indus valley the mature Harappan civilisation was in slow decline. Mohenjo-daro and Harappa were losing population, standardised weights and script were falling out of use, and by 1900 BCE the great planned cities were largely abandoned. Explanations range from climate change and river-course shifts to invasion or internal collapse; recent palaeoclimate work suggests the weakening of the Indian summer monsoon as a central factor. Successor communities scattered eastward toward the Ganges plains.

In north China the Erlitou culture, which many archaeologists identify with the semi-legendary Xia dynasty, had produced the earliest palace complexes and bronze ritual vessels in East Asia at its type site in Henan. Erlitou was giving way, in the late seventeenth century BCE, to the early Shang state centred first at Zhengzhou and later, most spectacularly, at Anyang. Cast-bronze ding vessels and rammed-earth walls were the technological signatures of the age.

In coastal Peru the great platform mounds of the Norte Chico civilisation had been abandoned two centuries earlier, and a variety of smaller successor cultures were filling the gap. In the Andes the Kotosh tradition, with its unique fire-hearth ritual chambers, continued at sites in the eastern foothills. In Mesoamerica maize agriculture was well established, but there was as yet no monumental architecture; the Olmec would not appear on the Gulf coast for another 350 years.

Highland New Guinea sustained the intensive taro and banana horticulture attested at Kuk Swamp, and stone-tool technologies had evolved for felling and processing tree crops. Coastal Melanesia was inhabited by Papuan-speaking populations. The Austronesian voyagers who would push out from Taiwan through the Philippines and into Near Oceania, carrying with them the distinctive dentate-stamped pottery of the Lapita tradition and eventually settling the whole of Remote Oceania, would begin their maritime expansion in only a few centuries' time.

figures.name: Hammurabi
figures.role: Sixth king of Babylon's First (Amorite) Dynasty; reigned 1792 to 1750 BCE and issued the law-stele in the last decade of his rule

figures.name: Shamash
figures.role: Mesopotamian sun-god and patron of justice, depicted on the top of the stele handing the symbols of rule to Hammurabi

figures.name: Shutruk-Nahhunte
figures.role: Twelfth-century BCE Elamite king who sacked Sippar and carried Hammurabi's stele to Susa as war booty

figures.name: Jacques de Morgan
figures.role: French archaeologist and director of the mission at Susa whose team uncovered the stele in three pieces in December 1901

figures.name: Vincent Scheil
figures.role: Dominican Assyriologist who published the first edition and French translation of the code in 1902
figures.credit: Association d'histoire et d'archeologie de Lorraine / Public domain

images.caption: The diorite stele of the Code of Hammurabi, c. 1754 BCE, in the Louvre. The relief at the top shows the king before the enthroned sun-god Shamash.

sources.title: Law Code of Hammurabi, King of Babylon
